summary: In these excerpts from 'Meditations on First Philosophy', René Descartes outlines his method of radical doubt, systematically discarding all beliefs based on the senses or complex reasoning due to the possibility of dreams or an evil deceiver. Through this rigorous process, he discovers the single indubitable truth that because he thinks and can be deceived, he must necessarily exist as a thinking thing.

principles.statement: Never place absolute trust in anything that has deceived you even once.
principles.rationale: Because the senses occasionally mislead us regarding minute or distant objects, prudence dictates that we cannot rely on them as a foundation for absolute certainty.
principles.source_quote: it is the part of prudence not to place absolute confidence in that by which we have even once been deceived.

principles.statement: To establish firm knowledge, one must demolish all previous opinions and rebuild from the foundation.
principles.rationale: Accepting false opinions early in life leads to a doubtful superstructure of knowledge; only a complete, foundational overhaul can establish certainty in the sciences.
principles.source_quote: convinced of the necessity of undertaking once in my life to rid myself of all the opinions I had adopted, and of commencing anew the work of building from the foundation, if I desired to establish a firm and abiding superstructure in the sciences.

principles.statement: I exist necessarily, so long as I am thinking.
principles.rationale: Even if an all-powerful demon is constantly deceiving me about the external world and my own body, the very act of being deceived requires that I exist to experience the deception.
principles.source_quote: Doubtless, then, I exist, since I am deceived; and, let him deceive me as he may, he can never bring it about that I am nothing, so long as I shall be conscious that I am something.

frameworks.name: Methodological Skepticism (The Method of Doubt)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When seeking to establish absolute, indubitable truth and a firm foundation for scientific or philosophical knowledge.
steps: Isolate yourself from practical cares and passions to focus entirely on knowledge rather than action.
Treat any belief that has even the slightest ground for doubt as if it were manifestly false.
Instead of examining every single belief, attack the foundational principles upon which all former beliefs rest (e.g., the senses).
Introduce extreme hypothetical scenarios (like a dreaming state or an evil demon) to test the limits of doubt.
Continue discarding doubtful beliefs until you find something absolutely certain, or until you prove that nothing is certain.
frameworks.source_quote: proceed by casting aside all that admits of the slightest doubt, not less than if I had discovered it to be absolutely false; and I will continue always in this track until I shall find something that is certain

mental_models.name: The Dream Argument
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating the reliability of sensory experience by recognizing that dreams perfectly mimic waking life, meaning current perceptions could be illusions.
mental_models.example: Descartes notes he has dreamt of sitting by the fire in his dressing gown while actually undressed in bed, concluding there are 'no certain marks by which the state of waking can ever be distinguished from sleep.'

mental_models.name: The Malignant Demon (Evil Genius)
mental_models.description: A thought experiment used to maintain rigorous doubt against deeply ingrained habits of belief. One imagines an all-powerful, deceitful being actively manipulating all perceptions and mathematical truths.
mental_models.example: Supposing the sky, air, earth, colors, and one's own body are merely illusions created by this demon to lay snares for credulity, forcing the thinker to suspend judgment on all external reality.

mental_models.name: The Archimedean Point
mental_models.description: An analogy for foundational epistemology. Just as Archimedes needed only one firm, immovable point to move the earth, a philosopher needs only one indubitable truth to build an entire system of knowledge.
mental_models.example: Using the absolute certainty of 'I am, I exist' as the single firm point to rebuild all knowledge after doubting everything else.

heuristics: Withhold belief from what is not entirely certain just as carefully as from what is manifestly false.
Attack the foundations of your beliefs rather than trying to disprove each belief individually.
If a belief relies on the body or the senses, discard it; if it relies only on thinking, keep it.

signature_quotes.text: it is the part of prudence not to place absolute confidence in that by which we have even once been deceived.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why he must doubt the information provided by his senses.

signature_quotes.text: I perceive so clearly that there exist no certain marks by which the state of waking can ever be distinguished from sleep, that I feel greatly astonished
signature_quotes.context: Introducing the Dream Argument to cast doubt on immediate sensory experiences.

signature_quotes.text: I will suppose, then, not that Deity, who is sovereignly good and the fountain of truth, but that some malignant demon, who is at once exceeding potent and deceitful, has employed all his artifice to deceive me
signature_quotes.context: Introducing the Evil Demon thought experiment to prevent himself from slipping back into habitual beliefs.

signature_quotes.text: Doubtless, then, I exist, since I am deceived; and, let him deceive me as he may, he can never bring it about that I am nothing, so long as I shall be conscious that I am something.
signature_quotes.context: Arriving at the first indubitable truth (the Cogito) despite the possibility of an evil deceiver.

signature_quotes.text: this proposition (pronunciatum) I am, I exist, is necessarily true each time it is expressed by me, or conceived in my mind.
signature_quotes.context: Formalizing the certainty of his own existence.

signature_quotes.text: I am therefore, precisely speaking, only a thinking thing, that is, a mind (mens sive animus), understanding or reason
signature_quotes.context: Defining the nature of the self after stripping away the body and senses.

anti_patterns.description: Trusting the senses implicitly as a source of absolute truth.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The senses occasionally mislead us regarding minute or distant objects, and we cannot definitively distinguish waking sensory experiences from vivid dreams.

anti_patterns.description: Allowing old, customary beliefs to perpetually recur and subdue one's judgment out of laziness or familiarity.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents the discovery of certain truth, keeping the mind trapped in probable but ultimately doubtful prejudices, much like a captive who dreads awakening from an agreeable dream.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to dismantle false opinions by dealing with each belief individually.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is an endless and impossible labor; it is much more efficient to remove the foundation, which necessarily involves the downfall of the whole edifice.
